LabComments: ~32 myomeres This larvae resembles Lepomis  & Pomoxis sp. Based on myomeres I will place it under Pomoxis, but pigmentation more in tuned with Lepomis. Time of year overlaps. So, will leave at Centrarchid for now. Will be vouchered
Similar to the one in sample 7976.9-2. D: 10/ A: 9

PreFlexion; 30/13 prepost myomeres, long preanal length approx 77%, but this might be due to tail-end shrinkage. VERY cyprinid in form. Pigment inconclusive. Too well-developed at size to be a Catostomid. Best left at Cyprinidae in the Sacramento R. system at this stage of development and condition.

FieldComments: Some veg got wrapped around flowmeter
LabComments: PostFlexion; 45/10 prepost myomeres and short preanal length. Distinctly a Clupeid and total myomere count is diagnostic.
PreFlexion; See previous taxon notes. One damaged specimen ID based on total myomere count and apparent gut length.
Common distinct taxa in Yolo Bypass samples.

Meristics consistent with M. berylina, which is a very common non-native silverside in Sacramento-San Joquin system. Prominent silver/dark stripe and scale cross-hatching is prominent, which generally distinguishes from other native more estuarine/marine topsmelt/jacksmelts.

FieldComments: Paddled drift tow (boat ran out of battery)

LabComments: Total myomere count appears to be 38-40. Small size at hatching and being inland in freshwater plus total myomere count is diagnostic. Common taxon in this project.
Low total myomere count of approx. 40 is distinguishes it from A. sapidissima at this inland site.
Low total myomere count of approx. 40 is distinguishes it from A. sapidissima at this inland site.

O. mykiss (steelhead/rainbow) and O. tshawytscha (Chinook) most common salmonids in Sacramento system.  Parr marks approx equal to space btn parr marks, 13-14 dorsal fin rays, and 19 anal fin rays fits chinook better than steelhead. Also, the small size of these specimens is not consisistent with steelhead that would be expected to be larger outmigrating smolts if collected at Yolo Bypass. More likely outmigrating Chinook fry.  Note: both specimens have what appear to be clean straight clips of caudal fin, which would be odd for marking fish of this size. Maybe facility damage or clipped for genetic testing when collected at facility?
Distinct taxon in project. IDd as YSL, but all specimens are tranistional late YSL and early PYSL.

LabComments: 63% PAL, 9 dors rays, 10 anal rays, apparent symmetrical caudal fin, small (terminial?) mouth, heavy pigment on occiput. Myomere counts appear to be approx  45 but somewhat obscured by pigment and development. Final ID based on total myomere count of 45 and pigmentation.  No caudal spot at length excludes blackfish and hitch and roach (as do other characters).
Very small at development, long PAL, total myomere counts approx 44. Small size at yolk absorption excludes Alosa and Clupea.
